![AI Model Routers Compared: Bifrost, LiteLLM, Portkey & More](https://i.mscdn.ai/blog-images/best-ai-model-routers-multi-provider-llm-cost.png?fm=auto&w=1200&fit=cover?fm=auto&w=1200&fit=cover)

## The Real Cost of Running AI Without a Router

If you’re building AI applications in 2026, you’ve probably noticed something: your LLM bills are getting out of hand. A customer support chatbot handling 10,000 conversations per day can burn through $7,500 monthly just on API calls. Scale that to 100,000 conversations and you’re looking at serious money.

The problem isn’t just cost. It’s the entire infrastructure mess that comes with using multiple AI providers. You hardcode OpenAI’s API format into your app. Then Anthropic releases a better model for coding tasks. Now you’re rewriting code. Then OpenAI has an outage and your entire service goes down.

AI model routers solve this. They sit between your application and LLM providers, automatically selecting the right model for each task based on complexity, cost, and performance. Smart routing can cut your LLM spending by 30-85% while maintaining response quality.

Here’s what actually matters when choosing an AI model router for production use.

## What AI Model Routers Actually Do

An AI model router acts like a traffic controller for your LLM requests. Instead of sending every prompt to one expensive model, it analyzes each request and routes it to the most suitable option from your model pool.

Simple question about your return policy? Route it to GPT-4o-mini at $0.50 per million tokens. Complex legal analysis requiring deep reasoning? Send it to Claude Opus at $15 per million tokens. The router makes these decisions in milliseconds.

The core components include:

- **Request Classification:** Analyzes prompt complexity, intent, and requirements
- **Model Selection:** Chooses the optimal model based on your criteria
- **API Management:** Handles authentication and format conversion across providers
- **Fallback Logic:** Switches to backup providers during outages
- **Cost Tracking:** Monitors spending across all providers in real-time

RWORK ORDER · NO. 0001ACCEPTED 09:42

YOU ASKED FOR

Sales CRM with pipeline view and email integration.

✓ DONE

REMY DELIVERED

Same day.

yourapp.msagent.ai

AGENTS ASSIGNEDDesign · Engineering · QA · Deploy

![Remy](https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy/lockup-h-sm.svg)The world's most powerful product manager agent [Try Remy today](https://mindstudio.ai/remy)

But there’s more to it than basic routing. Production-grade routers need semantic caching, load balancing, and comprehensive observability.

### How Intelligent Routing Actually Works

The routing process happens in four steps:

**1\. Feature Extraction**

The router analyzes your prompt and extracts features like task complexity, context length, domain specificity, and whether it needs tool calling. This happens using lightweight models or embedding-based classifiers.

**2\. Model Scoring**

Each available model gets scored based on its strengths for the extracted features. A coding task scores high for models trained on code. A creative writing task scores differently.

**3\. Cost-Performance Optimization**

The router balances quality requirements against cost constraints. If you need 95% accuracy and multiple models can deliver that, it picks the cheapest one.

**4\. Execution and Learning**

The request goes to the selected model. Advanced routers track the result quality and use this feedback to improve future routing decisions.

The key difference between basic and advanced routers is how much they learn from actual usage patterns versus relying on static rules.

## Why You Actually Need an AI Model Router

Enterprise LLM spending hit $8.4 billion in 2025, up from $3.5 billion in 2024. Most of that money is wasted on using expensive models for simple tasks.

### Cost Reduction That Actually Matters

Research from UC Berkeley and Canva shows that intelligent routing delivers 85% cost reduction while maintaining 95% of GPT-4 performance. But the real savings come from three specific optimizations:

**Semantic Caching**

Users ask the same questions in different ways. “What’s your refund policy?” and “How do I get my money back?” mean the same thing. Semantic caching recognizes this and serves cached responses for semantically similar queries.

Production systems report 20-40% cache hit rates. For high-volume applications, this eliminates millions of redundant API calls. One customer support system reduced costs by 69% just by implementing semantic caching.

**Task-Appropriate Model Selection**

Not every query needs your most powerful model. Simple FAQ responses work fine with smaller models at 10% of the cost. Complex reasoning tasks justify premium models.

The savings compound. If 60% of your queries are routine and you route them to cheaper models, your average cost per request drops by half.

**Batch Processing and Rate Limit Management**

Routers can batch non-urgent requests and take advantage of batch discounts from providers. OpenAI offers 50% discounts for batch API usage. Smart routers automatically identify which requests can wait and batch them.

### Reliability You Can’t Get From Single Providers

OpenAI had three major outages in 2025. Anthropic had rate limiting issues during peak hours. If your application depends on one provider, these become your problems.

AI model routers provide automatic failover. When your primary provider returns errors or times out, requests instantly switch to backup providers. Your users never notice.

The fallback logic gets sophisticated. Routers track provider health metrics like success rates, latency, and error patterns. When issues emerge, traffic shifts before users experience problems.

### Flexibility to Use the Best Tool for Each Job

Different models excel at different tasks. GPT-4 handles complex reasoning. Claude Sonnet 4.5 is better for code generation. Gemini 3 Pro processes multimodal inputs effectively.

## Remy doesn't write the code.It manages the agents who do.

AGENTS ASSIGNED TO THIS BUILD

R

Remy

Product Manager Agent

Leading

Design

Engineer

QA

Deploy

Remy runs the project. The specialists do the work. You work with the PM, not the implementers.

![Remy](https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy/lockup-h-sm.svg)The world's most powerful product manager agent [Try Remy today](https://mindstudio.ai/remy)

Without a router, you’re locked into one provider’s strengths and weaknesses. With routing, you can use specialized models for specific tasks while maintaining a single integration point in your code.

This flexibility extends to testing. Want to evaluate a new model? Add it to your router’s model pool and A/B test it against your current setup. No code changes required.

## Essential Features for Production AI Model Routers

Marketing pages list dozens of features. Here’s what actually matters when you’re serving thousands of requests per second.

### Performance and Latency

Your router adds overhead to every request. At 5,000 requests per second, even small delays compound into serious problems.

Look for routers that add less than 50 microseconds of latency. Python-based solutions often struggle here. Go and Rust implementations perform better at scale.

The performance gap matters. During load testing, Python-based routers like LiteLLM start breaking down around 300-500 RPS. Compiled language implementations handle 5,000+ RPS without issues.

### Semantic Caching That Actually Works

Basic caching only works for exact matches. Semantic caching uses vector embeddings to identify similar requests regardless of wording.

Good semantic caching systems let you tune the similarity threshold. Set it at 0.9 for near-identical queries. Lower it to 0.8 for broader matching. The right threshold depends on your use case.

Cache segmentation matters for security. Multi-tenant applications need isolated caches per customer. The router should handle this automatically.

### Real-Time Cost Tracking and Budgets

You need to know what you’re spending in real-time, not when the monthly bill arrives.

Effective cost tracking shows:

- Cost per request
- Cost per endpoint or API key
- Cost per user or team
- Cost per model and provider
- Token usage broken down by input and output

Budget enforcement prevents runaway costs. Set spending limits per team, project, or time period. When limits approach, the router can switch to cheaper models or throttle requests.

### Comprehensive Observability

When things break at 3am, you need to understand what happened quickly.

Essential observability features include:

- Request-level tracing across the entire routing path
- Latency metrics (P50, P95, P99) per model and provider
- Error rates and patterns
- Cache hit rates
- Model selection explanations

The best routers integrate with existing monitoring tools like Prometheus, Grafana, and Datadog. You shouldn’t need to learn a new observability system.

### Governance and Access Control

Enterprise deployments need role-based access control, audit logging, and compliance features.

Key governance capabilities:

- SSO integration with SAML or OAuth
- API key management with fine-grained permissions
- Request filtering based on content
- Data residency controls for EU/GDPR compliance
- Audit trails showing who requested what and when

For regulated industries like healthcare and finance, these aren’t optional features.

## Top AI Model Routers for 2026

The market has matured significantly. Here are the solutions that actually work in production.

### 1\. Bifrost

Bifrost stands out for raw performance. Built in Go, it adds only 11 microseconds of overhead per request at 5,000 RPS. That’s 50x faster than Python-based alternatives.

**Best For:** High-throughput applications where latency matters

**Key Strengths:**

- Ultra-low latency routing
- Semantic caching with vector similarity
- Cluster mode with peer-to-peer synchronization
- OpenAI-compatible API
- Support for 250+ models across providers

**Limitations:**

Smaller ecosystem compared to established players. Less enterprise governance features out of the box.

## Other agents start typing.Remy starts asking.

YOU SAID"Build me a sales CRM."

REMY ASKS

01DESIGNShould it feel like Linear, or Salesforce?

02UXHow do reps move deals — drag, or dropdown?

03ARCHSingle team, or multi-org with permissions?

Scoping, trade-offs, edge cases — the real work. Before a line of code.

![Remy](https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy/lockup-h-sm.svg)The world's most powerful product manager agent [Try Remy today](https://mindstudio.ai/remy)

**Pricing:** Open source with self-hosted deployment

### 2\. LiteLLM

LiteLLM is the most popular open source option, but performance becomes a problem at scale.

**Best For:** Development and low-traffic applications (under 300 RPS)

**Key Strengths:**

- Easy to get started
- Large community and ecosystem
- Extensive provider support
- Good documentation

**Limitations:**

Python architecture struggles past 300-500 RPS. Users report memory leaks and latency spikes under sustained load. The codebase has grown messy as features accumulated.

**Pricing:** Open source, with managed cloud option

### 3\. Portkey

Portkey offers the most complete feature set but costs add up quickly at scale.

**Best For:** Teams that need comprehensive features and don’t mind paying for them

**Key Strengths:**

- Rich observability and analytics
- Prompt versioning and management
- Strong governance features
- Good multi-provider support

**Limitations:**

Expensive at scale. Data residency for EU customers costs thousands monthly. SSO and advanced features are charged extras.

**Pricing:** Starts free, scales to thousands per month for enterprise features

### 4\. TrueFoundry

TrueFoundry takes a different approach, offering full AI infrastructure management beyond just routing.

**Best For:** Organizations running complete AI platforms including training and deployment

**Key Strengths:**

- Complete MLOps platform
- Self-hosted deployment options
- Deep Kubernetes integration
- Cost optimization across entire AI stack

**Limitations:**

Heavier weight than pure routing solutions. Longer setup time. More infrastructure to manage.

**Pricing:** Enterprise only, contact for quote

### 5\. OpenRouter

OpenRouter aggregates models from multiple providers into one marketplace.

**Best For:** Quick prototyping and testing different models

**Key Strengths:**

- Instant access to 250+ models
- Simple pay-as-you-go pricing
- No infrastructure to manage
- Good for model comparison

**Limitations:**

Less control over routing logic. Limited enterprise features. Adds another vendor in your stack.

**Pricing:** Pay per token plus small markup

### 6\. Cast AI

Cast AI focuses on Kubernetes-native AI deployments with strong cost optimization.

**Best For:** Teams already using Kubernetes who want to run self-hosted models

**Key Strengths:**

- Native Kubernetes integration
- Self-hosted LLM support
- Cost monitoring dashboard
- Model comparison playground

**Limitations:**

Requires Kubernetes expertise. More focused on infrastructure than pure routing.

**Pricing:** Based on cluster size and usage

## How to Choose the Right AI Model Router

The best router depends on your specific requirements. Here’s how to evaluate your options.

### Assess Your Traffic Patterns

Start with numbers. How many requests per second do you handle now? What do you expect in six months?

Under 100 RPS: Most routers work fine. Pick based on features and ease of use.

100-500 RPS: Start thinking about performance. Test under realistic load.

500-2000 RPS: Performance becomes critical. Avoid Python-based solutions.

2000+ RPS: You need compiled language implementations with proven scale.

### Calculate Your Current LLM Costs

Pull your last three months of bills from all LLM providers. Break down costs by:

- Total monthly spend
- Cost per request
- Which models you use most
- What percentage of requests could use cheaper models

If 50% of your requests are simple queries currently using GPT-4, you could cut costs in half by routing them to GPT-4o-mini.

### Define Your Quality Requirements

What accuracy do you actually need? Customer support chatbots can tolerate more variation than legal document analysis.

✗VIBE-CODED APP

Tangled. Half-built. Brittle.

✓AN APP, MANAGED BY REMY

DEPLOYProduction-ready✓

Architected. End to end.

### Built like a system. Not vibe-coded.

Remy manages the project — every layer architected, not stitched together at the last second.

![Remy](https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy/lockup-h-sm.svg)The world's most powerful product manager agent [Try Remy today](https://mindstudio.ai/remy)

Set clear thresholds:

- Minimum acceptable accuracy
- Maximum acceptable latency
- Failure tolerance

These thresholds guide routing decisions. The router won’t sacrifice quality for cost savings beyond your limits.

### Consider Your Infrastructure

Where does the router run?

**Self-hosted:** More control, but you manage infrastructure. Works well if you already run Kubernetes or have DevOps capacity.

**Managed service:** Less control, but zero infrastructure management. Good for smaller teams or rapid deployment.

**Hybrid:** Some routers offer both options. Start with managed, move to self-hosted as you scale.

### Test Under Realistic Load

Don’t trust marketing benchmarks. Test with your actual traffic patterns.

Run load tests that simulate:

- Your typical request volume
- Your actual prompt lengths and complexity
- Peak traffic patterns
- Provider failures and fallback scenarios

Measure latency at P50, P95, and P99. The P99 number shows what your slowest users experience.

## Advanced Routing Strategies

Basic routing picks models based on static rules. Advanced strategies get smarter over time.

### Complexity-Based Routing

The router analyzes prompt complexity using multiple signals:

- Token count and sentence structure
- Domain-specific terminology
- Question type (factual vs reasoning vs creative)
- Context length requirements

Simple prompts under 100 tokens with straightforward questions route to lightweight models. Complex multi-step reasoning tasks route to premium models.

### Consensus Routing for Critical Tasks

For high-stakes decisions, send the same prompt to multiple models and aggregate responses.

Iterative Consensus Ensemble (ICE) loops three models together, having them critique each other until they reach consensus. This approach raises accuracy 7-15 points over single model performance.

The cost increases, but for applications where accuracy matters more than speed, consensus routing makes sense.

### Reinforcement Learning Routing

Advanced routers learn from outcomes. Track which models perform best for which query types, then adjust routing decisions based on actual results.

This requires logging response quality metrics. When you know Model A handles legal queries better than Model B, future legal queries automatically route to Model A.

### Cost-Aware Dynamic Routing

Budget constraints change throughout the month. Early in the billing cycle, you might use premium models more freely. As you approach budget limits, route more aggressively to cheaper options.

Some routers support dynamic cost thresholds. Set different routing rules for different cost situations.

## Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

Teams make predictable mistakes when implementing model routing. Here’s what to watch out for.

### Over-Complicated Routing Logic

The temptation is to create elaborate rules with dozens of conditions. This rarely works well.

Start simple. Route based on prompt length and maybe one or two domain signals. Add complexity only when you have data showing you need it.

### Ignoring Cache Invalidation

Semantic caching saves money, but stale cache entries cause problems. Implement time-based expiration and invalidation triggers.

For data that changes frequently, either skip caching or set short TTLs. For stable reference data, longer cache times work fine.

### Not Monitoring Model Performance

Provider models change over time. What worked last month might not work now. Track accuracy, latency, and cost for each model continuously.

![Remy](https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy/lockup-h-sm.svg)The world's most powerful product manager agent [Try Remy today](https://mindstudio.ai/remy)

Set up alerts for performance degradation. When a model’s accuracy drops below threshold, investigate or switch providers.

### Insufficient Fallback Testing

Fallback logic only matters when things break. Test it regularly.

Deliberately fail your primary provider in staging. Does the router switch seamlessly? Do users notice? How long does failover take?

### Poor Error Handling

Not all errors should trigger fallback. Rate limits, invalid requests, and authentication errors need different handling than provider outages.

Configure your router to distinguish error types and respond appropriately. Retry transient errors. Fail fast on invalid requests. Switch providers for availability issues.

## How MindStudio Handles AI Model Routing

MindStudio takes a different approach to multi-provider AI infrastructure. Instead of requiring you to set up and manage a separate routing layer, MindStudio’s platform includes intelligent model selection built into the workflow builder.

### Visual Model Selection

In MindStudio, you configure model routing through the visual workflow interface. Each AI step lets you specify which model to use, but you can also set conditional routing based on workflow context.

For example, set up a workflow where initial classification uses GPT-4o-mini, but complex queries that fail the classification step automatically escalate to Claude Opus. No custom code required.

### Built-in Cost Optimization

MindStudio tracks costs across all your AI steps automatically. The platform shows you which workflows consume the most tokens and suggests optimization opportunities.

You can set budget alerts at the workspace or workflow level. When spending approaches limits, MindStudio can automatically switch to more cost-effective models.

### Multi-Provider Support Without Complexity

MindStudio supports models from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and other major providers through one unified interface. Add your API keys once, then use any model in any workflow.

The platform handles authentication, rate limiting, and error handling across providers. When one provider has issues, MindStudio can automatically retry with an alternative.

### Testing and Optimization Tools

MindStudio’s evaluation tools let you compare different models on your actual use cases. Run the same prompts through multiple models, compare outputs side by side, and see costs for each option.

This makes it easy to find the right model for each task without extensive testing infrastructure.

### Enterprise Governance

For teams using MindStudio Teams or Enterprise, you get centralized control over which models teams can access, spending limits per team or project, and audit logs of all AI requests.

This governance layer prevents accidental overspending and ensures compliance with company policies.

## The Future of AI Model Routing

The routing landscape continues to evolve. Here’s what’s coming.

### Better Mixture-of-Experts Architectures

Models like Meta’s Llama 4 use mixture-of-experts architectures that activate only subsets of parameters per request. This approach is moving into routing systems.

Future routers might decompose complex queries into subtasks, route each to specialized models, then synthesize results. This enables more granular optimization than current all-or-nothing routing.

### Hardware-Aware Routing

As organizations deploy more self-hosted models, routers will need to consider hardware availability and utilization.

Route to local models when GPU capacity exists. Overflow to cloud providers during peak demand. Balance cost against latency based on current infrastructure state.

### Multimodal Routing Complexity

Most current routers focus on text. As applications handle images, video, and audio, routing gets more complex.

Different models excel at different modalities. The router needs to analyze input types, route accordingly, and handle format conversions between providers.

### Tighter Integration with Observability

The line between routing and monitoring blurs. Future routers will use observability data more directly in routing decisions.

Real-time latency tracking informs load balancing. Quality metrics from production traffic improve model selection. Cost data drives automatic optimization.

## Getting Started With AI Model Routing

Here’s a practical roadmap for implementing routing in your organization.

### Week 1: Assessment

Analyze your current LLM usage. Pull billing data. Categorize requests by complexity. Identify optimization opportunities.

Calculate potential savings if you route 40% of requests to models that cost 80% less.

### Week 2-3: Tool Selection

Test 2-3 routing solutions with your actual traffic patterns. Focus on performance under realistic load.

Implement in a staging environment. Send duplicate traffic to test the router while production continues unchanged.

### Week 4: Pilot Deployment

Route a small percentage of production traffic. Start with 5-10%. Monitor closely.

Compare response quality, latency, and cost against your baseline. Tune routing rules based on results.

### Week 5-8: Gradual Rollout

Increase the percentage of routed traffic weekly. Add more sophisticated routing rules as you learn what works.

Target 50% of traffic routed by week 6, 100% by week 8.

### Ongoing: Optimization

Review routing performance monthly. Look for new optimization opportunities as your traffic patterns change.

Test new models as they become available. Provider capabilities and pricing change frequently.

## Key Takeaways

AI model routing is no longer optional for production applications. The economics and reliability requirements make it essential.

- **Cost reduction is real:** Smart routing cuts LLM spending 30-85% while maintaining quality
- **Performance matters:** Choose routers that add minimal latency at your traffic volume
- **Start simple:** Basic routing based on prompt complexity delivers most of the value
- **Test under load:** Marketing benchmarks don’t reflect your reality
- **Monitor continuously:** Model performance changes over time

The router you choose depends on your scale, budget, and technical requirements. For high-throughput applications, compiled language implementations like Bifrost perform best. For rapid prototyping, managed services like OpenRouter get you started quickly. For comprehensive AI platforms that include routing, solutions like MindStudio eliminate the need to piece together multiple tools.

The key is getting started. Every day you run without intelligent routing costs you money and limits your flexibility. Pick a solution, test it with a small percentage of traffic, and scale from there.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### What’s the difference between an AI gateway and an AI model router?

AI gateways provide standardized API access and infrastructure management across providers. They handle authentication, rate limiting, and basic request routing. AI model routers go further by making intelligent decisions about which model to use for each request based on cost, performance, and quality requirements. Most modern solutions combine both capabilities.

### How much can I actually save with intelligent routing?

## How Remy works.You talk. Remy ships.

Build me a sales CRM with a pipeline view and email integration.

Scoping the project

✓ Live at yourapp.msagent.ai

![Remy](https://www.mindstudio.ai/remy/lockup-h-sm.svg)The world's most powerful product manager agent [Try Remy today](https://mindstudio.ai/remy)

Savings depend on your traffic patterns and current model usage. If you’re using GPT-4 for all requests, including simple ones, you can typically save 30-50% by routing routine queries to cheaper models. Organizations with high cache hit rates on semantic caching report additional 40-70% reductions. Total savings of 60-85% are achievable with aggressive optimization.

### Will routing affect response quality?

Not if configured properly. The router only selects cheaper models when they can meet your quality requirements. Set minimum accuracy thresholds and the router won’t sacrifice quality for cost. Production systems routinely maintain 95%+ of their original quality while cutting costs in half.

### Do I need a router if I only use one AI provider?

Even with one provider, routing between different model sizes saves money. OpenAI offers multiple variants of GPT models at different price points. Routing simple queries to GPT-4o-mini instead of GPT-4 cuts costs significantly. You also gain reliability benefits from easier provider switching when you eventually add alternatives.

### How difficult is it to implement a model router?

Implementation complexity varies. Managed services like OpenRouter take minutes to set up. Self-hosted solutions like LiteLLM require more infrastructure work but give you more control. The bigger challenge is tuning routing logic to match your specific use case, which typically takes 2-4 weeks of testing and optimization.

### Can I use multiple routers together?

You can layer routers for different purposes. For example, use one router for basic provider abstraction and failover, and another for advanced features like semantic caching. However, this adds complexity and latency. Most teams are better served by choosing one comprehensive solution.

### What happens when my primary model provider has an outage?

Good routers detect provider failures in milliseconds and automatically switch to configured backup providers. Your users experience no downtime. The router continues monitoring the primary provider and switches back when service restores. Configure fallback chains with 2-3 backup options for maximum reliability.

### How do I measure if routing is working?

Track three key metrics: average cost per request before and after routing, response quality scores on a test set, and P95/P99 latency. You should see 30%+ cost reduction with minimal quality impact and latency increase under 50ms. Most routers provide dashboards showing these metrics automatically.

### Should I route at the application level or use a centralized gateway?

Centralized gateways work better for most organizations. They provide one place to manage routing logic, enforce budgets, and gather observability data across all applications. Application-level routing makes sense only if different apps have wildly different requirements that can’t be expressed in shared routing rules.

### What about data privacy and compliance?

This depends on where your router runs and which providers you use. Self-hosted routers can keep all data within your infrastructure. Managed routers typically see your prompts and responses, which may create compliance issues for sensitive data. Check router security practices and data retention policies carefully, especially for GDPR, HIPAA, or financial regulations.

# Architectural Patterns for Text-to-SQL: Leveraging LLMs for Enhanced BigQuery Interactions

[![Arun Shankar](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*vEPGpdaRi4Ms10NymQypiw.png)](https://medium.com/@shankar.arunp?source=post_page---byline--59756a749e15---------------------------------------)

[Arun Shankar](https://medium.com/@shankar.arunp?source=post_page---byline--59756a749e15---------------------------------------)

Follow

36 min read

·

Nov 11, 2023

981

13

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D59756a749e15&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fgoogle-cloud%2Farchitectural-patterns-for-text-to-sql-leveraging-llms-for-enhanced-bigquery-interactions-59756a749e15&source=---header_actions--59756a749e15---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

> **TLDR:** This article deep dives into the Text-to-SQL domain, demonstrating the growing reliance on Large Language Models (LLMs) for this complex task. It emphasizes the synergistic relationship between LLMs and Google’s BigQuery, showcasing how this partnership improves SQL query generation across various applications. The article explains how LLMs act as cognitive processing engines that accurately interpret user requests and interface with BigQuery to generate precise SQL commands. This combination simplifies data acquisition for users and presents a scalable framework for database interactions that can be adopted across different sectors. A case study from the travel and leisure industry is explored, showing the integration of this technology in managing travel bookings. The article examines five distinct **_architectural patterns_** for Text-to-SQL, discussing their pros and cons, and outlining their best use-case scenarios. It also provides practical demonstrations using [notebooks](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures), complete with datasets and executable code to showcase each pattern.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/0*J4kixp-RV8WGiJBC)

Photo Credit: [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

## Introduction

Text-to-SQL is a task in natural language processing (NLP) that aims to automatically generate Structured Query Language (SQL) queries from natural language text. This task involves converting text input into a structured representation and using it to generate a semantically correct SQL query that can be executed on a database. The task presents multitude of challenges. At its core, the difficulty arises from the inherent differences between human language, which is fluid, ambiguous, and context-dependent, and SQL, which is a precise and structured language.

Historically, the problem has been viewed as a **query reformulation** task, employing sequence-to-sequence models and adopting deep neural architectures. These models were strategically trained to transmute natural language into corresponding SQL expressions utilizing the intricate mappings between the two.

Nonetheless, these architectures introduced their own array of challenges. Primarily, there was a need to generate and curate datasets that comprised pairs of human language interrogations and their corresponding SQL statements for pre-training or fine-tuning. Moreover, both the input (natural language queries) and the output (SQL queries) commonly necessitated normalization. This demanded both pre-processing and post-processing stages, intensifying the complexity of the process. It also implied that a substantial volume of training data was indispensable. The incorporation of custom domain knowledge, such as table names, schema, and column descriptions, also presented a formidable challenge.

Before the introduction of LLMs for Text-to-SQL, user queries had to be pre-processed to match a specific template. This template was then used to rephrase the user query. However, these templates were typically use-case specific, making them difficult to extend beyond that. As a result, a considerable amount of data preparation and manual work was required to achieve the necessary format and template.

With the emergence of LLMs, the Text-to-SQL landscape has undergone a significant transformation. Demonstrating exceptional performance, LLMs are capable of generating accurate SQL queries from natural language descriptions, a feat made possible by their vast knowledge base and context understanding capabilities. Their complex architectures, coupled with comprehensive data used for pre-training, allow them to decipher complex relationships between words, thereby overcoming many challenges that were present in previous methods. As the evolution of LLMs progresses, their proficiency in this field is expected to further increase, heralding a new era for natural language interfaces to databases. One of the key advantages of LLMs is the elimination of the need to curate and refine training datasets from natural language to queries.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/0*eRKnPqJJwG9eFKVE)

Photo Credit: [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

In our upcoming demonstration, we will be harnessing the capabilities of the **PaLM 2** model from Google, a state-of-the-art addition to the lineage of LLMs with an emphasis on Text-to-SQL. Our focal point will be a flight reservation system, a complex domain with intricate relationships and data structures. Within this system, we’ll explore various tables encompassing flight details, passenger information, booking history, and more, all hosted within BigQuery datasets. Leveraging PaLM 2’s LLM-driven architecture patterns, we aim to showcase the seamless transformation of natural language inquiries into precise SQL queries. This will not only highlight the model’s efficacy in understanding and processing human language but also its adeptness in navigating relational databases and producing accurate, actionable SQL outputs. Through these examples, we will experience firsthand the evolution of Text-to-SQL capabilities and the potential of LLMs in real-world applications.

[BigQuery](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery) is Google’s fully-managed data warehouse service, BigQuery, offers a powerful platform for large-scale data analytics. It supports quick SQL queries and is highly valued for its performance, scalability, and ease of data integration, making it essential for data-driven enterprises.

Querying within a flight reservation system presents a myriad of challenges, primarily due to the multifaceted nature of the data involved. Flight systems encompass a vast array of interconnected tables and datasets, ranging from customer details and ticketing information to flight schedules and pricing metrics. Leveraging BigQuery for analytics further compounds the complexity, as analysts aim to harness its power for deep dives into data, trend forecasting, and real-time insights. A particular point of concern is the significant time and effort required by data engineers, scientists, and other specialists in crafting the necessary SQL queries.

The creation of these queries is not a simple, linear task. Instead, it often involves a cascading chain of SQL commands, each building upon the other, to aggregate, group, filter, and dissect the data in various ways to extract meaningful insights. The slicing and dicing of data, especially in a system as intricate as flight reservations, demands meticulous attention to detail and a deep understanding of both the data’s structure and the business’s needs. As a result, professionals often find themselves dedicating hours, if not days, to perfecting their queries and establishing efficient data pipelines to ensure the smooth flow and processing of information.

Diving into a practical example, let’s consider the intricacies of querying within a system containing four tables for simplicity: `reservations`, `customers`, `transactions`, and `flights`. Each table, while valuable on its own, gains significance when interconnected with others. For instance, a query aiming to identify the most frequent fliers might need to join the customers table with reservations and flights, cross-referencing passenger details with flight bookings. Further complexity arises when trying to discern which of these frequent fliers have made the most transactions in the last quarter, necessitating a merge with the transactions table.

Each query scenario described above involves multiple joins, aggregations, filters, and possibly sub-queries. Crafting these queries demands not only SQL expertise but also a profound understanding of the underlying data relationships and business objectives. The potential combinations and insights that can be derived from these four tables are vast, and each query can become an intricate task, underscoring the challenges inherent in such systems.

## Text-to-SQL: The Architectural Imperative

Translating natural language to SQL is vital for efficient data management and retrieval. A well-structured architecture improves the accuracy and speed of these translations, enhancing the capabilities of LLMs in handling complex user queries.

It’s important to understand the pros and cons of specific patterns within this architecture. Their effectiveness depends on the context, and knowing where to apply them can optimize Text-to-SQL conversions. Adapting these patterns to fit unique scenarios can lead to better SQL generation.

## Google Cloud Platform (GCP) - GenAI Glossary

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/0*90yhlI_XPJZ8lW71)

Photo Credit: [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=medium&utm_medium=referral)

[Vertex AI](https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai) is Google Cloud’s suite designed to simplify and integrate the ML workflow for teams, aiming to accelerate the development and deployment of AI models. It stands out for its ability to automate and optimize ML tasks on a secure and scalable infrastructure.

`PaLM 2` is a state-of-the-art language model developed by Google with improved multilingual, reasoning, and coding capabilities. It is trained on multilingual text, spanning 100+ languages, which has significantly improved its ability to understand, generate, and translate nuanced text across a wide variety of languages.

The foundational models we will use in this post are part of Google’s suite of generative AI tools. These models are optimized for various tasks and are based on PaLM 2. Each model serves as a strategic building block in the AI ecosystem, designed to boost productivity and innovation across a range of applications.

`Text Bison`: A model fine-tuned to follow natural language instructions and is suitable for a variety of language tasks.

`Chat Bison`: A model fine-tuned for multi-turn conversation use cases. It is designed to provide consolidation, suggestions, and help with problems in a chat-like format.

`Code Bison`: A model fine-tuned to generate code based on a natural language description of the desired code.

`Code Chat Bison`: A model fine-tuned for chatbot conversations that help with code-related questions. It is a foundation model that supports multi-turn conversations specialized for code.

The main difference between `Code Bison`and `Code Chat Bison`is their intended use. `Code Bison`is designed for code generation tasks that require a single interaction, while `Code Chat Bison`is optimized for back-and-forth interactions in a continuous conversation format. `Code Chat Bison`is more suitable for code tasks that are completed with multiple interactions, such as code debugging or problem-solving.

In addition to `PaLM 2`-based foundation models available as APIs, Vertex AI offers a range of generative AI tools. These include Vertex AI Search and Conversation, Generative AI Studio, and Model Garden. Each tool has its unique features:

- `Vertex AI Search and Conversation`: This tool enables developers to construct generative AI-powered search and chat experiences. It offers ready-to-use capabilities for building and deploying chatbots and search apps quickly. Developers can also incorporate multiple features into their workflows through API integration or console.
- `Generative AI Studio`: This Google Cloud console allows for rapid prototyping and testing of generative AI models. Developers can use it to test models using prompt samples, design and save prompts, adjust foundation models, and convert between speech and text via rich UI.
- `Model Garden`: Model Garden houses over 100 cutting-edge LLMs and task-specific models. This includes Google’s own first party models discussed above, and open-source models like `Llama 2`, TII’s `Falcon`, `Stable Diffusion`, `Mistral` and more. It streamlines the process of finding, deploying, and maintaining foundation models for developers via both API and console.

In this article, we’ll focus on the first-party foundation models from Google Cloud — `Code Bison` and `Code Chat Bison`.

## Architectural Patterns

The evolution of LLM-based applications in database management and query formulation has opened new avenues for data interaction. By integrating LLMs into the process of generating SQL queries from natural language, we can streamline and enhance data retrieval processes. Here, we outline **five** distinct patterns for implementing LLMs in SQL query generation.

## Pattern I: Intent Detection and Entity Recognition with Text-to-SQL

In traditional Natural Language Understanding (NLU) systems, the process of converting text to SQL queries begins with intent detection. This step is crucial as it discerns the user’s purpose from their query or utterance, which is particularly relevant in applications like chatbots. Intent detection is typically approached as a multi-class classification problem, requiring a supervised learning model that is trained on a dataset balanced across all possible intents.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Intent Detection and Entity Recognition with Text-to-SQL](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*ZP16Qn4x7X1C9BogmgpxOw.png)

Intent Detection and Entity Recognition with Text-to-SQL

However, the introduction of LLMs has changed the landscape. These models can perform tasks like intent detection in a zero-shot or few-shot manner, eliminating the need for extensive training data.

Another key component of traditional text-to-SQL systems is Named Entity Recognition (NER), which involves identifying and extracting entities from the user’s input. Recognizing these entities is essential as they often correspond to database elements like table names or column values, which are integral to formulating the SQL query.

LLMs excel at NER and contextual understanding, enabling them to identify entities within the text effectively. This capability allows for the seamless integration of these entities into SQL query templates.

Now, let’s explore this first pattern and see how it works, as illustrated above. This pattern builds on traditional systems that normally involve intent and NER components, but here, it uses an LLM instead.

1. **Intent Detection**: The user’s query or utterance is input into the LLM, which determines the user’s intent. For example, the intent behind a query in the context of booking a flight reservation. Sample prompt completion pairs used for few-shot prompting of the LLM for intent classification are shown below. Traditionally, this would have been performed by a supervised fine-tuned classifier or a rule engine.

```
Need all the bookings from 10th to 15th October 2023.,RETRIEVE_RESERVATIONS
Who made a reservation last Wednesday?,IDENTIFY_RECENT_CUSTOMERS
What was our earning from confirmed bookings in October 2023?,CALCULATE_REVENUE
"In 2023, which month witnessed the highest number of departures?",FIND_PEAK_DEPARTURE_MONTHS
How many customers fall into five distinct age categories?,GROUP_AND_COUNT_CUSTOMERS_BY_AGE
```

2. **Entity Recognition**: The same user input is analyzed by the LLM, either concurrently or sequentially, to perform NER — the extraction of relevant entities from the text. Below, we show example pairs used for prompting.

```
"Can you show me all the reservations from October 10th to October 15th, 2023?","Start Date:October 10th, 2023|End Date:October 15th, 2023"
"What bookings do we have from 10/10/2023 to 10/15/2023?","Start Date:10/10/2023|End Date:10/15/2023"
"Show the reservations occurring between the second week of October 2023.","Start Date:October 8th, 2023|End Date:October 15th, 2023"
"List all bookings that are happening from October 10 to 15, 2023.","Start Date:October 10, 2023|End Date:October 15, 2023"
"Fetch the reservations from the second week of October 2023.","Start Date:October 8th, 2023|End Date:October 15th, 2023"
```

3\. **Mapping Intent to Database Tables**: The detected intent is used to determine which database tables to query. While LLMs can assist in this step, it can also be programmatically treated as key-value mapping retrieval. A sample mapping is shown below.

```
RETRIEVE_RESERVATIONS,reservations|flights
IDENTIFY_RECENT_CUSTOMERS,reservations|customers
CALCULATE_REVENUE,reservations|transactions
FIND_PEAK_DEPARTURE_MONTHS,flights
GROUP_AND_COUNT_CUSTOMERS_BY_AGE,customers
```

4\. **Schema Filtering**: Post identification, the schemas of the relevant tables are retrieved, setting the stage for SQL generation.

5\. **SQL Statement Construction**: The gathered information, which includes the user query, detected intent, and extracted entities, is integrated with mapped tables and filtered schema info, such as table and column descriptions. This comprehensive data info is compiled into a structured prompt for the LLM as shown below. This assists the LLM in generating an accurate SQL statement in response to the user’s query.

```
Please construct a SQL query using the information provided below:

Input Parameters:
-----------------
INTENT: {intent}
EXTRACTED_ENTITIES: {entities}
MAPPED_TABLES: {tables}

User Query:
-----------
{user_query}

Table Schemas:
--------------
{filtered_table_schemas_text}

Note:
- Please prefix the table names with `flight_reservations`.
```

Here’s an example of a user query and the corresponding SQL statement generated by the LLM:

```
Provide a list of all flight reservations from October 10th to October 15th, 2023
```

```
SELECT *
FROM flight_reservations.reservations
WHERE reservation_datetime BETWEEN '2023-10-10' AND '2023-10-15'
```

6\. **SQL Execution**: The generated SQL statement is executed against the database, completing the text-to-SQL conversion process.

7\. **Human-Friendly Output**: Optionally, the output from the SQL query can be returned to the LLM to be reformatted into a more human-readable format, thereby improving the user experience. This is particularly useful if you want to provide a response as part of a chat application or any conversational interface.

The sample query for this step is as follows:

```
User's Question:
----------------
{user_query}

BigQuery Result:
----------------
{bq_response}

Task:
-----
Please convert the above query result into a human-readable format.

IMPORTANT Notes:
----------------
- The response should be courteous and human-friendly.
- If the answer doesn't require a tabular structure, avoid using it.
```

In a nutshell, pattern I streamlines the complexities typically encountered in text-to-SQL systems. This results in a solution that is not only more efficient but also easier to design. To see this pattern applied in practice, a sample notebook with the relevant code is available for reference. It can be accessed at this GitHub [location](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/01-Pattern-I/00-llm-flow.ipynb). The repository also contains all necessary support files, such as prompt completion pairs for few-shot prompting, templates, and additional resources, located in the [data directory](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/tree/main/data).

Please note that before you proceed with executing the notebook for Pattern I, it is important to set up the required BigQuery dataset and tables. This can be done by running the setup notebook found in the ‘00-Setup’ directory, specifically `00-create-tables.ipynb`. This preparatory step is crucial for the successful implementation of all patterns covered in this article.

**Advantages:**

- Suitable for small BigQuery datasets with a limited number of tables and columns.
- Ideal for replacing existing intent detection and NER systems with LLM-based solutions.
- Simplifies the pipeline by eliminating the need for multiple model training and re-training pipelines.
- Appropriate for client-facing applications or systems with a limited range of scenarios.
- Aims for high accuracy in SQL query generation and offers explainability.
- Facilitates easier debugging and inspection of individual modules or components.

**Limitations:**

- Expansion to include new scenarios requires manual updates to the data used for few-shot prompting.
- Lacks the flexibility to automatically address novel query scenarios without manual intervention.

## Pattern II: LLM Flow with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

If we’re dealing with hundreds or even thousands of tables in BigQuery, or instances where tables are broad with thousands of columns, the previous pattern doesn’t hold up. It’s practically impossible to map the detected intent to the corresponding tables. The same issue arises when we face new scenarios or query types, the first pattern can’t reliably map intent to the correct tables, or filter schema descriptions and columns when dealing with large scales. So, we need a more efficient method, which is where pattern II comes in.

Essentially, pattern II leverages RAG. We turn the table descriptions and schema descriptions (columns) into embeddings, then index these embeddings for search. Using semantic search, we encode the user’s query into an embedding and compare it with the indexed embeddings to find matching tables and columns. This strategy trims down the search space, allowing us to pick the top K results. The result is a system that can operate at scale, automatically approximating the candidates needed for our SQL generation. Let’s break it down step by step.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![LLM Flow with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*y7yymeqzxhkRCfC60_PLKA.png)

LLM Flow with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

1. **Embedding and Indexing Descriptions:** Start by encoding the table and column descriptions into embeddings using a text embedding model. For example, you can use the Vertex AI’s text embedding model `textembedding-gecko`. Once the descriptions are encoded, create two indices — one for table descriptions and another for column descriptions. A standalone vector index such as `FAISS` can be utilized here, considering this is simply a demonstration.

Standalone vector indices greatly enhance the search and retrieval of vector embeddings, but they lack some features found in vector engines or databases. Vector engines are tools that allow efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors. They are designed to search within vector sets of any size, even those that do not fit in RAM, thus making them suitable for large-scale applications with billions of vectors. These engines revolve around the concept of vector embeddings, which are vector data representations carrying crucial semantic information for AI to understand and maintain [long-term memory](https://medium.com/itnext/remembering-conversations-building-chatbots-with-short-and-long-term-memory-on-aws-c1361c130046) when executing complex tasks.

Google Cloud provides `Vertex AI Vector Search`, formerly known as `Vertex AI Matching Engine`, which is a high-scale, low-latency vector database. It is designed to handle large amounts of data at low latency, making it suitable for applications requiring efficient vector similarity-matching or approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) services. `Vertex AI Vector Search` can be used for a variety of purposes, such as implementing recommendation engines, search engines, chatbots, and text classification. It supports searching from billions of semantically similar or related items, and the use of embeddings is not limited to words or text, but can also be applied to images, audio, video, and user preferences.

2\. **Query Vectorization and Table Matching**: In the second stage of the process, the user’s query is converted into a vector representation using the designated encoding model. This encoded query is then compared against a pre-indexed repository of table descriptions to identify relevant tables that align with the user’s intent. The resultant matches are sorted by relevance, with a focus on the top five matches, as determined by setting the parameter K to 5.

3\. **Column Discovery and Metadata Integration:** Proceeding to the third step, a similar procedure is applied, but this time the query’s vector is matched against an index of column descriptions. This match-making process yields a set of relevant columns, each accompanied by metadata and the corresponding table information. In this phase, the parameter K is set to 20 to expand the search and provide a broader selection of potential matches.

By integrating the outcomes of the second and third steps, particularly in scenarios involving multiple BigQuery datasets, a multi-layered search strategy is employed in this architectural pattern. This three-tiered approach — consisting of separate searches for datasets, tables, and columns — allows for a comprehensive and refined matching process, pinpointing the most appropriate candidates for query construction.

4\. **SQL Query Synthesis:** In the next step, focused on SQL generation, the system synthesizes all identified components into a cohesive SQL query. The formulation of this query is guided by the LLM, which translates the user’s initial request into an accurate SQL statement. A sample template for such a query is provided below, illustrating the process of systematic query assembly.

```
Given the following inputs:
USER_QUERY:
--
{query}
--
MATCHED_SCHEMA:
--
{matched_schema}
--
Please construct a SQL query using the MATCHED_SCHEMA and the USER_QUERY provided above.
The goal is to determine the availability of hotels based on the provided info.

IMPORTANT: Use ONLY the column names (column_name) mentioned in MATCHED_SCHEMA. DO NOT USE any other column names outside of this.
IMPORTANT: Associate column_name mentioned in MATCHED_SCHEMA only to the table_name specified under MATCHED_SCHEMA.
NOTE: Use SQL 'AS' statement to assign a new name temporarily to a table column or even a table wherever needed.
```

5\. **SQL Execution**: The generated SQL statement is executed against the database, completing the text-to-SQL conversion process.

6\. **Human-Friendly Output**: Optionally, the output from the SQL query can be passed back to the LLM to be reformulated into a more human-readable format, enhancing the user experience.

**Advantages:**

- Suitable for processing large datasets and wide tables in BigQuery.
- Simplifies workflow by collapsing intent detection, NER, and manual mapping into semantic search.
- Reduces inference costs due to shorter, more efficient prompts.
- Prunes unnecessary data, focusing on relevant table and column descriptions.
- Enhances scalability of data processing tasks.

**Limitations:**

- Adds complexity to the pipeline through the need for semantic search.
- Requires careful consideration of embedding strategies and parameters for effective search.
- One issue is that the tool often retrieves irrelevant tables and columns. This leads to complex queries and unnecessary joins, which increase computation time and result in lengthy, unneeded responses.
- Given that the initial stage of this approach is essentially a search, it suffers from common information retrieval issues such as poor recall and precision. It may require mitigation strategies like re-ranking to highlight the most relevant results.

A sample notebook demonstrating the RAG pattern for Text2SQL can be located [here](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/02-Pattern-II/00-llm-flow-with-rag.ipynb).

## Pattern III: Using SQL Agents

Agents are advanced AI systems that expand the capabilities of LLMs to perform a broad spectrum of tasks that surpass basic text generation. At their core, these agents are designed for interactive communication, reasoning, and task execution. They operate through detailed prompts that define personas, provide instructions, grant permissions, and give context. These prompts shape the agent’s responses and guide their actions.

At its core, agents harness the sophisticated language abilities of LLMs to function either autonomously or semi-autonomously, responding to human prompts. They are equipped with a range of tools, including calculators, code interpreters, APIs, and search engines, allowing them to acquire information and perform actions to complete their designated tasks, thereby establishing themselves as more dynamic than simple language processors. The key components of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system are: 1/ its ability to plan, 2/ decompose tasks into subgoals, 3/ select the appropriate API for invocation, and 4/ craft responses based on the results provided by these APIs.

Moreover, these agents have the capacity to analyze SQL databases by transforming natural language queries into SQL commands. The `LangChain` SQL agent for BigQuery exemplifies this capability, enabling users to interact with BigQuery databases using natural language. `LangChain` itself is an open-source framework that utilizes LLMs for processing natural language and carrying out a diverse array of tasks, demonstrating the multifaceted utility of these intelligent systems.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Using SQL Agents](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*c-IKDAVl3OnIRylyzs-kXw.png)

Using SQL Agents

Here’s a step-by-step guide on how to use LLM agents to interact with SQL data, as illustrated in the above diagram:

1. **Establishing a Database Connection**: Begin by establishing an ODBC connection with the Simba Google BigQuery connector. Make sure you have the appropriate credentials and permissions, which can differ based on the language model agent and the database in question. After securing these requirements, you can proceed to pass the user’s question or utterance directly to the agent for processing.
2. **Schema Inference and Configuration**: Configure essential parameters such as the catalog and dataset name, and authenticate using OAuth with the driver and service account key credentials. This configuration is crucial for the LLM agent to understand the database schema.
3. **Natural Language Query Processing**: Once the agent is launched, users can input natural language queries. The agent interprets these queries to generate the corresponding SQL commands.
4. **Query Execution**: The LLM agent executes the SQL commands, autonomously accessing tables and columns to construct and retrieve query results.
5. **Traceback and Self-Correction**: In cases where an LLM-generated query is syntactically incorrect, a traceback occurs. The agent is equipped with the ability to use this feedback to self-correct, ensuring that the query execution process is smooth and efficient.
6. **Human-Friendly Output Formatting**: To enhance the user experience, the output from the SQL query is reformulated into a more human-readable format by the LLM agent, providing a clear and concise presentation of the data.

For the above scenario, in the initial query attempt, there was an issue with the column name `customer_id` being ambiguous because it exists in both `customers` and `reservations` tables, and the query did not specify which table to use it from. This led to an error message indicating that the SQL query could not be processed because of the ambiguity in column reference.

Upon recognizing the error, the query was revised to fully qualify the column names by prefixing them with the appropriate table aliases (`c` for `customers` and `r` for `reservations`). This is a common practice in SQL to avoid ambiguity when dealing with multiple tables that have columns with the same name. The corrected query specified that `customer_id` should come from the `customers` table, which resolved the issue and allowed the query to be executed successfully, retrieving the list of customers with recent flight reservations.

**Advantages:**

- Greatly simplifies user tasks.
- Can be swiftly implemented to address a variety of scenarios.
- The system learns and enhances its performance from setbacks.
- Particularly effective for databases with uniform schemas and recognized query operations.

**Limitations:**

- It can be a bit tough for a standard agent to navigate the intricacies of database architectures.

- LLMs have a context window that restricts the amount of text it can handle. This might pose a problem for extensive databases.

- Agents often encounter some issues when dealing with SQL databases, such as making up tables and fields not present in the database.

A sample Jupyter notebook that demonstrates how to set up the BigQuery SQL agent and test its text-to-SQL capabilities across six different flight reservation scenarios is captured in this [notebook](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/03-Pattern-III/00-BQ-agent.ipynb).

## Pattern IV: Direct Schema Inference with Self-Correction

Inspired by our prior pattern in which an agent autonomously inferred the database schema, rectified errors, and ultimately formulated an effective SQL strategy, Pattern IV revisits the core mechanics of this method while enhancing customizability and explainability.

This approach begins with the direct inference of the schema, utilizing a ‘ _seed prompt_’ that instructs the LLM to construct an SQL query corresponding to a user’s inquiry. The execution of this initial prompt continues iteratively until it meets with success. Failures are treated as critical learning opportunity for the LLM, allowing it to scrutinize tracebacks and utilize error messages to refine and evolve the seed prompt into an improved query iteration. This self-corrected query is then tested; if successful, the process is complete; if not, the iterative cycle of refinement and testing is sustained until a successful outcome is secured.

The distinctiveness of this pattern lies in the granular control it offers over the initial seed prompt template, the self-corrective prompt evolution mechanism, and vital process parameters such as the number of permissible retries, all of which can be fine-tuned to enhance efficiency.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Direct Schema Inference with Self-Correction](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*AR03XQPoBTvosviRB0uAdA.png)

Direct Schema Inference with Self-Correction

Let’s break down the steps as per the above architecture illustration:

1\. We begin with the user query (question) and a seed template as follows:

```
Please craft a SQL query for BigQuery that addresses the following QUESTION provided below.
Ensure you reference the appropriate BigQuery tables and column names provided in the SCHEMA below.
When joining tables, employ type coercion to guarantee data type consistency for the join columns.
Additionally, the output column names should specify units where applicable.\n
QUESTION:
{}\n
SCHEMA:
{}\n
IMPORTANT:
Use ONLY DATETIME and DO NOT use TIMESTAMP.
--
Ensure your SQL query accurately defines both the start and end of the DATETIME range.
```

2\. **Schema Inference**: The LLM takes the table names inputted by the user and infers the database schema. This step is crucial for understanding the relationships and structure of the data. Unlike the SQL agent pattern, which automated this step, there is a manual aspect involved where the user selects the tables to focus on. The BigQuery client is utilized to infer the schema directly from the chosen tables.

3\. **Query Generation**: With the inferred schema, the LLM generates the initial SQL query. It involves filling in the blanks in a seed template with the necessary elements. This process occurs behind the scenes, where the LLM constructs the SQL statement based on the provided template.

4\. **Error Handling and Self-Correction:** If the initial query execution fails, the LLM captures the error messages and uses them to modify the query. The error feedback and the incorrect SQL statement guide the LLM to evolve the query. The LLM continues to iterate on the seed prompt, refining it until the query executes successfully.

5\. **Execution and Exit**: The process concludes successfully when the evolved query executes without errors. At this point, the system stops further iterations and exits the query generation loop.

Here is an example of an evolution prompt, which expands the original seed prompt with each iteration.

```
{prompt}
Encountered an error: {msg}.
To address this, please generate an alternative SQL query response that avoids this specific error.
Follow the instructions mentioned above to remediate the error.

Modify the below SQL query to resolve the issue:
{generated_sql_query}

Ensure the revised SQL query aligns precisely with the requirements outlined in the initial question.
```

**Advantages:**

- The implementation is straightforward as it relies on direct schema inference, making the system intuitive to set up.
- Optional descriptions for tables and columns mean that the system can function with minimal metadata, offering flexibility.
- High explainability is a key benefit; one can track prompt evolution and error codes to understand LLM’ responses and troubleshoot issues.
- Customizability is enhanced over previous models, allowing adjustments to prompts, evolution criteria, and the number of retries to fit specific needs.

**Limitations:**

- The process may involve several iterations, which could introduce execution overhead, affecting performance.
- Manual selection of tables for schema inference could be labor-intensive and less efficient than automated processes especially when trying to handle new and unseen scenarios.
- Using seed prompts involves incorporating the full schema, a process that can be resource-intensive due to the context window limitations of language models. This issue becomes more pronounced when table and column descriptions, along with sample data, are added — particularly for categorical and string data types. Moreover, as the prompt evolves through the addition of error messages and previous incorrect attempts, the risk of exceeding the context window capacity increases, thereby posing a potential challenge to the model’s ability to effectively manage and process the increasingly large input with each retry operation.

Here is a sample [notebook](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/04-Pattern-IV/00-self-correct.ipynb) illustrating Pattern IV. It demonstrates self-correction across the six scenarios we previously tested with Agents (Pattern III). This example employs `Code Bison` as the cognitive function of this setup.

**Variation to the Schema Inference Pattern**:

The current pattern’s drawback is the expansion of the seed prompt with each iteration, incorporating error messages and the previously generated, incorrect SQL queries. This expansion can lead to increased costs for subsequent calls to the LLM due to the growing input size.

To mitigate this issue, a strategic adaptation is to replace the standard code-generating model (`Code Bison`) with a conversational code model, such as `Code Chat Bison`. This approach reframes the iterative process as a dialogue, wherein error messages and incorrect SQL statements are passed along with simple instructions in a conversational turn to the code model. This effectively circumvents the issue of an expanding prompt, thus preserving the context window capacity and reducing the risk of running out of context during evaluation, which is particularly beneficial in scenarios with numerous retries.

In the evolution prompt below, we are not prefixing the original prompt with the previous state. Instead of expanding the seed prompt as we were doing previously, we are only passing the error message and the generated SQL query in the turn as part of the chat session.

```
prompt = f"""Encountered an error: {msg}.
To address this, please generate an alternative SQL query response that avoids this specific error.
Follow the instructions mentioned above to remediate the error.

Modify the below SQL query to resolve the issue:
{generated_sql_query}

Ensure the revised SQL query aligns precisely with the requirements outlined in the initial question."""
```

The refined evolution prompt, following this optimization, maintains a concise and focused input stream for the LLM. The effectiveness of leveraging `Code Chat Bison` for a self-correction mechanism is demonstrated in an example notebook available in our shared GitHub repository [here](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/04-Pattern-IV/01-self-correct-chat.ipynb).

### An Examination of Self-Correction

Let’s delve into a specific example, Scenario 6, from the shared notebook to illustrate how this approach adeptly self-corrects, ultimately generating an accurate BigQuery SQL for the stated question.

```
Identify and rank all customers aged 18+ who have `Confirmed` reservations for the current month, ordered by their age. Make sure to display their ages in the result.
```

```
ATTEMPT: 1
--------------------------------------------------
WITH current_month AS (
SELECT DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH) AS start_of_month,
         DATE_ADD(DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH), INTERVAL 1 MONTH) AS end_of_month
)

SELECT c.customer_id,
       c.first_name,
       c.last_name,
       DATEDIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth) / 365 AS age,
       r.reservation_id,
       r.status
FROM current_month
CROSS JOIN flight_reservations.customers c
JOIN flight_reservations.reservations r
ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
WHERE r.status = 'Confirmed'
AND r.reservation_datetime BETWEEN current_month.start_of_month AND current_month.end_of_month
AND DATEDIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth) / 365 >= 18
ORDER BY age
--------------------------------------------------
FAILED
====================================================================================================
ATTEMPT: 2
--------------------------------------------------
SELECT DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH) AS start_of_month,
         DATE_ADD(DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH), INTERVAL 1 MONTH) AS end_of_month
)

SELECT c.customer_id,
       c.first_name,
       c.last_name,
       DATE_DIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth, YEAR) AS age,
       r.reservation_id,
       r.status
FROM current_month
CROSS JOIN flight_reservations.customers c
JOIN flight_reservations.reservations r
ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
WHERE r.status = 'Confirmed'
AND r.reservation_datetime BETWEEN current_month.start_of_month AND current_month.end_of_month
AND DATE_DIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth, YEAR) >= 18
--------------------------------------------------
FAILED
====================================================================================================
ATTEMPT: 3
--------------------------------------------------
WITH current_month AS (
SELECT DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH) AS start_of_month,
         DATE_ADD(DATE_TRUNC(CURRENT_DATE(), MONTH), INTERVAL 1 MONTH) AS end_of_month
)

SELECT c.customer_id,
       c.first_name,
       c.last_name,
       DATE_DIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth, YEAR) AS age,
       r.reservation_id,
       r.status
FROM current_month
CROSS JOIN flight_reservations.customers c
JOIN flight_reservations.reservations r
ON c.customer_id = r.customer_id
WHERE r.status = 'Confirmed'
AND r.reservation_datetime BETWEEN current_month.start_of_month AND current_month.end_of_month
AND DATE_DIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth, YEAR) >= 18
ORDER BY age;
--------------------------------------------------
SUCCEEDED
CPU times: user 86.8 ms, sys: 21.3 ms, total: 108 ms
Wall time: 16 s
```

Let’s break down the attempts to understand how the system is self-correcting its SQL query:

### Attempt 1

### **Goal**:

The query aims to select customers with confirmed flight reservations for the current month who are at least 18 years old.

### **Issues**:

- The calculation of age is incorrect. It uses `DATEDIFF` to find the difference in days between the customer's date of birth and the end of the current month, and then divides by 365. This approach is imprecise for calculating age in years.
- The syntax for defining the `current_month` Common Table Expression (CTE) is correct, but the query failed, likely due to the age calculation method.

### Attempt 2

### Adjustments:

- **Age Calculation**: Replaced the age calculation method with `DATE_DIFF(current_month.end_of_month, c.date_of_birth, YEAR)`. This directly computes the age in years, which is more accurate.

### **Issue**:

- The query starts with a `SELECT` statement outside the CTE without the `WITH` keyword, causing a syntax error.

### Attempt 3

### Final Corrections:

- **Syntax Correction**: Corrected the syntax by adding the `WITH` keyword at the beginning of the query to properly define the `current_month` CTE.
- **Successful Execution**: This query successfully retrieves the desired data: customers with confirmed reservations in the current month who are at least 18 years old, ordered by age.

Thus, it is observable that the system self-corrected by initially enhancing the age calculation method and subsequently rectifying the syntax error. In its final successful attempt, it effectively combined accurate age computation with the proper SQL structure, thereby attaining the intended query result.

## Pattern V: Direct Schema Inference, Self-Correction & Optimization

Let’s examine the final pattern we want to cover. This pattern builds on the previous one with one key difference: instead of stopping after a successful execution, we continually refine the process. We ask the system to optimize the query for latency and stop only after reaching a set number of iterations. Essentially, this pattern involves continual self-correction and optimization.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

![Direct Schema Inference, Self-Correction & Optimization](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*pVbnhRc2K2a1L4wXAdul6Q.png)

Direct Schema Inference, Self-Correction & Optimization

The steps are similar to those in Pattern 4, with additional optimization measures. This includes a Query Optimization loop, which refines the query continuously, even after successful execution, to improve performance. There’s also a focus on latency improvement by reducing the query execution time.

**Note:** In our revised approach here, we’ve made a notable departure from the previous pattern. We’ve adjusted the temperature parameter from 0 to 1, a change that significantly impacts the model’s operation. In the past (Pattern IV), the temperature was set to 0, which resulted in deterministic model output, i.e., the same input consistently led to the same output, leaving little room for variation or creativity. We’ve enhanced this by dynamically evolving the prompt with the inclusion of error codes and generated queries. With the temperature now at 1, the behavior of the model is significantly altered. It now allows for more creative and improvisational responses. The model is no longer strictly tied to the most likely response, but instead explores a broader range of possibilities. This adds an element of unpredictability or ‘randomness’ to the model’s behavior, which is advantageous in generating more diverse and innovative responses. This approach is designed to optimize the feedback loop without the need for suffixing error codes.

**Advantages**:

- Enhances query efficiency and speed, optimizing for dynamic improvements.
- Suitable for high-performance settings where query execution time is a priority.

**Limitations**:

- Incurs higher costs due to multiple executions involving several calls to the LLM, increasing the expense per call.
- Slower process as it requires waiting for the execution and ranking of all candidates, which cannot be parallelized due to the necessity of previous state information.
- No assurance that the fastest query will produce accurate results, necessitating result evaluation.

A demo notebook covering this pattern can be found in the shared repository accompanying the article [here](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/05-Pattern-V/00-self-correct-rank-by-latency.ipynb).

**Variation to the Self-correct and optimize pattern:**

Similar to what we observed in Pattern IV, where we replaced `Code Bison` with `Code Chat Bison` to change the prompt expansion strategy to a conversational strategy, we can do the same here. An example notebook covering Pattern V using `Code Chat Bison` can be found [here](https://github.com/arunpshankar/LLM-Text-to-SQL-Architectures/blob/main/05-Pattern-V/01-self-correct-rank-by-latency-chat.ipynb).

### An Examination of Self-Correction and Self-Optimization

Let’s delve into a scenario (Scenario 1 from the shared notebooks above) involving a flight reservation, utilizing the LLM for self-correction alongside query optimization. The LLM undergoes several SQL query iterations, each aimed at enhancing performance and rectifying errors. This procedure is characterized by a series of trials, with each subsequent attempt refining the query based on the outcomes of the previous ones. The following is a detailed analysis of each trial to provide an understanding of this progressive process.

```
Provide a list of all flight reservations from October 10th to October 15th, 2023
```

```

--------------------------------------------------
SELECT
